coredata.dc:title: Surface state engineering of carbon dot/carbon nanotube heterojunctions for boosting oxygen reduction performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Baohua
An, Guangbin
Chen, Jia
Guo, Huazhang
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Platinum-based (Pt) catalysts are the most common commercial catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions (ORR). Unfortunately, their high price, scarcity and poor durability hinder their further development. Therefore, the development of effective and economical ORR electrocatalysts has received increasing attention. Here, carbon dots (CDs) enriched in amino functional groups were successfully loaded onto carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with a large surface area and helical structure through a surface state engineering strategy. The resulting composites (CD/CNTs) are 0D/1D nano heterojunction structures. The CD/CNTs showed superior ORR activity compared with CNTs and CDs (Eoneset = 0.95 V, E1/2 = 0.81 V and limiting current density = 4.74 mA cm−2). In addition, the stability of CD/CNTs in an alkaline medium was up to 30000 s. The excellent ORR performance of CD/CNTs can be attributed to the dominant role of amino-N, the synergistic effect of heterojunctions formed by CDs and CNTs, and the high Lewis basicity. The composite electrocatalyst synthesized by the CD-regulated CNT strategy is expected to be a reliable cathode candidate for future energy conversion devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon nanotubes
0D/1D heterojunction
Oxygen reduction reactions
Durability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis procedure and morphology analysis of synthesized catalyst
ORR performance of the synthesized catalyst
Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) test of the synthesized catalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and lateral size distribution of CDs. (b) and (c) HRTEM images of CDs. (d) TEM image of CNTs. (e) and (f) HRTEM image of CD/CNTs. (Fig. 1c and Fig. 1f are local magnified views of the box selection in Fig. 1b and Fig. 1e, respectively. The yellow circle in Fig. 1e represents monodisperse CDs.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CNTs and CD/CNTs, and (d-f) XPS survey spectra, High-resolution XPS N 1 s spectra and High-resolution XPS C 1 s spectra of CDs, CNTs and CD/CNTs.

(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of CNTs and CD/CNTs (The inset is the corresponding pore size distribution). (b) BET surface area and total pore volume of CNTs and CD/CNTs. (c) Contact angle images of CNTs and CD/CNTs.

(a) CV curves, (b) RDE LSVs of CDs, CNTs, CD/CNTs, and 20 % Pt/C in 0.1 M KOH. (c) Tafel plots of CDs, CNTs, CD/CNTs, and 20 % Pt/C. (d) Nyquist plots of CNTs, CD/CNTs and 20 % Pt/C. (e) LSV curves of CD/CNTs at different rotation speeds at a scan rate of 10 mV/s (f) Chronoamperometric curves of CD/CNTs and 20 % Pt/C at 0.70 V vs RHE potential for 8 h. (all tests are in the O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution).
(a) O2-TPD and (b) CO2-TPD profiles of CDs and CD/CNTs.
Schematic Illustration of the Synthetic Processes of CD/CNTs.

head.ce:title: Surface state engineering of carbon dot/carbon nanotube heterojunctions for boosting oxygen reduction performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Platinum-based (Pt) catalysts are the most common commercial catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions (ORR). Unfortunately, their high price, scarcity and poor durability hinder their further development. Therefore, the development of effective and economical ORR electrocatalysts has received increasing attention. Here, carbon dots (CDs) enriched in amino functional groups were successfully loaded onto carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with a large surface area and helical structure through a surface state engineering strategy. The resulting composites (CD/CNTs) are 0D/1D nano heterojunction structures. The CD/CNTs showed superior ORR activity compared with CNTs and CDs (Eoneset = 0.95 V, E1/2 = 0.81 V and limiting current density = 4.74 mA cm−2). In addition, the stability of CD/CNTs in an alkaline medium was up to 30000 s. The excellent ORR performance of CD/CNTs can be attributed to the dominant role of amino-N, the synergistic effect of heterojunctions formed by CDs and CNTs, and the high Lewis basicity. The composite electrocatalyst synthesized by the CD-regulated CNT strategy is expected to be a reliable cathode candidate for future energy conversion devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is considered the main electrochemical process in fuel cells [1,2]. The 4-electron (4e-) transfer process in the ORR greatly determines the reaction rate for fuel cells [3]. To date, the sluggish reaction rate is still the main technological bottleneck in the improvement of fuel cells. Generally speaking, rare pure Pt and its alloys are commercial ORR catalysts because they can provide enough ORR kinetics to improve the reaction speed [4,5]. Unfortunately, their high cost, scarcity and poor durability severely limit the large-scale commercial applications of precious metal catalysts [6–9]. Therefore, nonnoble metal (Ni, Co, Mn, and Fe) catalysts have been widely investigated in ORR electrocatalysis [10–12]. However, the unideal reaction activities of these catalysts hinder their potential applications in these renewable energy technologies [13]. Finding efficient and sustainable ORR electrocatalysts remains a challenge.

With the extensive research on these ORR catalysts, carbon nanomaterials, such as high conductivity carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have appeared and been shown to gain ORR catalysis capacities for energy conversion and storage [14–17]. However, the ORR electrocatalytic activity of pure CNTs is not ideal, which reveals many problems that need to be addressed [18]. For instance, it is difficult to control the activity due to relatively few active sites [19–21]. The previously impressive experimental and theoretical results have demonstrated that the ORR performance of carbon nanomaterials can be improved through various innovative molecular and/or nanostructure modification strategies, including surface functionalization, composite doping, and heteroatom doping [22–24]. Conventional doping methods by ammonia or small molecular dopants under high-temperature conditions usually destroy the one-dimensional (1D) structure of CNTs and reduce their electrical conductivity despite the improvement of their ORR activity [25]. Exploring moderate treatments plays a role in achieving a balance between the ideal structure and high activity.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of quantum dot with zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures smaller than 10 nm [26–31]. Due to their unique physical and chemical properties, an increasing number of previous reports have proven that the edge effect of CDs plays an important role in electrocatalysis because the electrocatalytic reaction occurs at its rich edge sites rather than its substrate plane [32–37]. Meanwhile, CDs containing different functional groups on their surfaces can be easily functionalized together with other nanomaterials for electrocatalysis applications [38,39]. Our previous work reported the synthesis of two-dimensional metal–organic skeleton nanosheets induced by carboxylated CDs to improve their electrocatalytic activity [33]. Based on the above advantages, CDs are feasible candidates to regulate the catalytic properties of CNTs.

Here, we designed a simple two-step hydrothermal strategy through surface state engineering to synthesize a highly efficient and excellent composite catalyst of monodisperse CDs anchored on CNTs (denoted as CD/CNTs). The CDs with a large number of amino functional groups dispersed on CNTs significantly promoted the ORR catalytic performance of the composites. This method does not damage the structure of the carbon nanotubes themselves, but the introduction of amino electron-donating functional groups on CNTs greatly increases the electron cloud density. Meanwhile, the conjugated structure makes the material possess excellent electrochemical stability. Notably, the CD/CNTs catalysts showed better ORR activity and stability than 20 % Pt/C. Our discovery provides a simple way to develop low-cost, environmentally friendly and efficient ORR electrocatalysts.

Ferric citrate (FeAC), urea, glutamic acid (99 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), stearoyl chloride (98 %), pyrrole (99 %), ammonium persulfate (99 %), and petroleum ether (99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., ltd. Ethanol (98 %), and hydrochloric acid (37 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents used in this study were analytical grade.

The CDs were prepared by a previously reported one-pot solvothermal method [33]. Typically, 0.25 mg of ferric citrate (FeAC) and 0.5 mg of urea were dissolved in deionized water (DIW, 50 mL) and dispersed uniformly in the solution by ultrasound. Then the solution was transferred to an autoclave made of Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. When the temperature drops to room temperature, the solution is filtered out with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the unreacted solids. The solution was then concentrated with a rotary evaporator and dialysis with a dialysis bag for one week. Finally, the solid CDs powder after dialysis was placed in a 60 °C oven to dry for the following characterization.

The prepared CNTs were synthesized by the methods reported in the previous literature [40]. Typically, dissolve 3.53 g glutamic acid in a mixture of DIW (14 mL), acetone (12 mL) and NaOH (1.92 g) to maintain the solution pH = 12. Then 6.05 g stearyl chloride and 10 mL NaOH solution (0.2 mol/L) were slowly added to the above solution at the same time, and the pH of the solution was maintained at 12 for 1 h. Hydrochloric acid was added dropwise to the above-obtained solution to adjust the pH = 1. Then, the solid was washed with DIW until pH = 7. Finally, washing with petroleum ether several times, and freeze-drying to obtain precursor polypyrroles (p-PPys).

In a typical synthesis, 0.0245 g of p-PPys was dissolved in methanol (12 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Then, pyrrole (0.166 mL) and DIW (60 mL) were added to the above solution respectively. Then, 0.548 g ammonium persulfate was dissolved in DIW (1.2 mL), and then the solution was added to the above-mixed solution, and the resulting mixture was then stirred for 30 min. After washing the black solid with ethanol and DIW several times, it was dried in an oven at 60 °C. After that, the obtained polypyrroles (PPys) is placed in a tubular furnace at a rate of 5 °C min−1 at 800 °C under N2 flow for 6 h. When the reaction is slowly cooled to room temperature, chiral CNTs were obtained.

The synthesis method of CD/CNTs is similar to that of CDs. Specifically, 0.05 g of CNTs was added to previously prepared filtered solids CDs solution (50 mL). The mixed solution is fully stirred by ultrasonic so that CNTs are uniformly dispersed in the CDs solution, and then the solution was transferred to an autoclave made of Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 1 h. When the temperature drops to room temperature, the black solid obtained after solution filtration was dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain CD/CNTs.

The characterization tools for catalyst testing, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Raman spectroscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) are based on the test instruments used in our previous published work [9]. The measurements were made using the Autolab Electrochemical workstation. All the electrochemical testing procedures also refer to our previously published articles [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis procedure and morphology analysis of synthesized catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed process of the surface state engineering synthesis catalyst is shown in Scheme 1 . FeAC and urea were used to prepare CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The helical CNTs were synthesized by pyrolysis of PPys precursors (See the preparation method for details). Then, the CNT powder was added to the CD solution and subjected to the secondary hydrothermal process to construct the architecture of CDs and CNTs. After the postsynthetic method, the obtained material was a black powder and denoted as CD/CNTs (Fig. S1). CNTs are usually applied as substrates to enhance the surface area for anchoring and dispersing active CDs and improve mass transport during electrocatalysis [41].

The morphology of CDs and CD/CNTs was observed by TEM in Fig. 1 . The CDs are nearly monodispersed, and their lateral sizes are between 2 and 4 nm, with an average size of 3 ± 0.5 nm in Fig. 1a. The high-resolution TEM image of CDs further proves this view of their size. It can be seen more clearly and intuitively from Fig. 1b and Fig. S2 a that each CD is highly crystalline, which clarifies that the synthesized CDs are endowed with a high degree of graphitic structure. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. S2 b, the well-defined lattice stripes of the CDs can be seen after simply locally enlarging the TEM image of Fig. 1b, which indicates that the sample obtained quite high crystallinity. Its lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the facet (100) of graphite [26,27,30].

Fig. S3 shows the TEM image of PPys. Owing to the strong electrostatic interaction, PPys can be effectively induced by chiral surfactant molecules to form ordered helical structures at the molecular level [40]. Moreover, the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping of PPys demonstrates that the C, N, and O elements are uniformly dispersed on PPys (Fig. S4). Subsequently, the CNTs are synthesized by pyrolysis of PPys at 800 °C. The ordered helices of PPys can be transferred to CNTs after pyrolysis [40]. The corresponding TEM image shows that CNTs have a relatively rough surface structure and well-distributed helical morphology, with a diameter of approximately 80 nm (Fig. 1d and Fig. S5). Similarly, the EDS images of CNTs also show the uniform distribution of C, N, and O (Fig. S6). The CNTs with helical shapes are arranged in an array and contain many voids between such structures, which provides convenient conditions for anchoring CDs. The TEM image of CD/CNTs can confirm the above hypothesis. As shown in Fig. 1e and Fig. S7, the CDs are uniformly dispersed on CNTs without agglomeration, and a nano heterojunction is formed between the CDs and CNTs. Fig. 1f is a magnified view of the frame selection in Fig. 1e. The lattice size of CDs is 0.21 nm, while maintaining good crystallinity, which confirms that the introduction of CDs into CNTs will not damage their structures. Compared with the TEM image of CNTs, the surface of the CD/CNTs is rougher (Fig. S7), which indirectly proves that CDs were successfully anchored on CNTs.

The structural characteristics of the samples are shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD patterns of CDs, CNTs, and CD/CNTs were used for phase analysis and crystallinity determination. As shown in Fig. 2a, the XRD spectrum of CDs shows a sharp diffraction peak at 25.6°, which confirms the high crystallinity of CDs in TEM images. The shifting of the 2θ value corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon is attributed to the multidomain that has less effective π–π interaction arising from some sp3 bonding [34]. The CNTs and CD/CNTs have a similar diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.2°. Furthermore, CNTs and CD/CNTs show a weaker peak shape at 40-50° corresponding to the crystalline graphitic (101) plane of the carbon material, which indicates that the dispersion of the CDs on the CNTs did not modify the crystal structure of the CNTs. Raman spectroscopy is an effective method to study the crystal defects of carbon materials with nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak at approximately 1350 cm−1 is assigned to the D band, which is attributed to the introduction of defects and the breakdown of translational symmetry, whereas the G band appearing at approximately 1590 cm−1 corresponds to the sp2 lattice of graphene, which is related to the ordered structure of the surface of the materials [42]. Therefore, the intensity ratio ID /IG of the D and G bands can be used to analyze the degree of defects in the carbon materials. The ID /IG of CDs, CNTs and CD/CNTs are 0.60, 0.87, and 0.90, respectively, which reveals that the addition of CDs with a relatively high defect degree onto CNTs only slightly ranges from the graphite degree of CNTs. The phenomenon of the Raman results confirms that CD-decorated CNTs can maintain the structure of CNTs and reserve their electrical conductivity.

The chemical bonds and molecular structure of the catalysts were measured by FT-IR and XPS. All samples contain five FT-IR characteristic absorption peaks (Fig. 2c). The stretching vibrations of the CC/CC, CO, CO and CN groups at 1621, 1130, 1562, and 1448 cm−1 are all observed, which are further clarified by XPS. In addition, an obvious signal at approximately 3400–3200 cm−1 can be seen in the FTIR spectra of CDs and CD/CNTs, which belongs to free amino functional groups [43,44]. This phenomenon shows that the introduction of CDs can indeed realize amine functionalization of CNTs. Notably, the CD/CNTs display the absence of the CO group. It may be that during the hydrothermal recombination process, the amino functional group was introduced onto the CNTs, resulting in the cleavage of the CO bond.

The elemental composition and state of the catalyst were revealed by XPS. Fig. 2d shows three main signals, including C, N and O, without any other Fe signals, which is consistent with the XRD results. From the survey spectra, the N content of CDs was 12.15 at%. With the introduction of CDs into CNTs, the N content increased from the original 10.54 at% of CNTs to 15.74 at% of CD/CNTs (Table S1), which demonstrates that the addition of CDs is beneficial to strengthening the N level of the composite. The N 1 s spectrum of CD (Fig. 2e) is deconvoluted into four N species, namely, pyridine-N (397.8 eV), amino-N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.5 eV), and graphite-N (401.5 eV). Among the four N species, amino-N and graphite-N are the dominant state in CDs. Differing from the N species of CDs, CNTs do not possess an amino-N group and the pyrrolic-N is dominant out of the four N species, which is in agreement with the CNT precursors [40]. To our surprise, in the N 1 s spectrum of CD/CNTs there are only three N species; the pyrrolic-N species is absent. Interestingly, the binding energy of the amino-N of CD/CNTs is significantly shifted compared with that of CDs; in sharp contrast, the amino-N in CD/CNTs increases from 0 (CNTs) to 78.69 at%, which also shows that CDs are successfully introduced into CNTs. The increase in pyridine-N and the decrease in pyrrolic-N/graphitic-N in CD/CNTs may be due to the transformation and decomposition of unstable N species during the hydrothermal process [10]. In the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2f), the CDs and CNTs have four peaks, which are attributed to C C/CC, C O, C N and CO (284.80, 285.65, 286.83, and 288.73 eV). In contrast, the CD/CNTs contain only three peaks without a CO bond, which is consistent with the results of the FT-IR data. The O 1 s spectra of all three samples (Fig. S7) showed two peaks corresponding to the CO/OH bond (533.7 eV) and the CO bond (531.1 eV) [10]. Overall, with the inducement of CDs in CNTs, the structure of CNTs is unchanged, and the surface functional groups are drastically modified. The increase in amino groups on the surface of CD/CNTs can enhance the electrocatalytic active sites by strengthening its electron cloud densities [45–47].

To further understand the microstructure of the samples, the CNTs and CD/CNTs were tested by N2 isotherm adsorption–desorption experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the adsorption curves of both exhibited typical type IV isotherms, demonstrating their mesoporous structures [48]. The ​​CNTs display a large BET surface area as high as 228.1 m2 g−1 and a total pore volume of 1.14 cm3 g−1 (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the average pore size was located at 80 nm, which can be observed from the inset in Fig. 3a. The PPys induced monomers to form helical structures during the polymerization of pyrrole, resulting in the large surface area and abundant mesopores of CNTs [49]. Furthermore, the CD/CNTs have a higher BET surface area of ​​342.2 m2 g−1 than CNTs and a similar total pore volume of 1.18 cm3 g−1 with CNTs, which revealed that the addition of CDs into CNTs can enlarge the active sites on the CNTs. The possible reason for this phenomenon is that the CDs with lone pair electrons formed a conjugated structure with the large π bond of the outer wall of CNTs, which increases the surface area of the obtained heterojunction [40]. CD/CNTs with large surface areas have more active sites and faster mass transfer rates in the electrocatalytic process [50]. Thus, the CD/CNTs are expected to gain excellent ORR activity. The wettability of CNTs and CD/CNTs was further evaluated by contact angle measurements. As shown in Fig. 3c, the contact angle of CNTs is 105.1°. When CDs were introduced to CNTs, the hydrophilicity of the amino functional groups on the surface of CD/CNTs increased. Therefore, the contact angle of CD/CNTs dropped sharply to 69.5°. Meanwhile, the porous structure of CD/CNTs is also conducive to the rapid absorption of water droplets, which also confirms the improvement of hydrophilicity. Since the hydrophilicity determines the mass transfer rate of O2 dissolved in solution from solution to the active site [51–53], it can be reasonably expected that CD/CNTs catalysts can show excellent ORR activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR performance of the synthesized catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The ORR is a key electrochemical reaction in the cathode of a fuel cell and is usually evaluated by several factors, such as the onset potential (Eoneset), half-wave potential (E1/2) and limiting current density [54]. To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of the synthesized catalyst for the ORR, we used a three-electrode system to detect relative parameters in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte solution saturated with O2. For comparison, we measured a 20 % Pt/C electrode under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CDs exhibit an ORR current peak at 0.52 V vs RHE. After regulating CNTs with CDs, the measured ORR current peak of CD/CNTs shifted to 0.8 V vs RHE, which is superior to that of CDs and similar to that of 20 % Pt/C. This enhancement is mainly due to the fast mass transport behavior from CDs to electronegative CNTs [55]. In Fig. 4b, the Eoneset, E1/2, and limiting current density of the CD/CNTs electrode are 0.95 V, 0.81 V, and 4.74 mA cm−2, respectively, which are more positive than those of CDs (0.67 V, 0.58 V, and 1.49 mA cm−2), CNTs (0.85 V, 0.75 V, and 4.51 mA cm−2) and close to 20 % Pt/C catalysts (0.98 V, 0.83 V, and 5.01 mA cm−2). In terms of Eoneset and E1/2, the CD/CNTs exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity among the as-prepared catalysts. Furthermore, the Tafel slope is the polarization of the electrode reflecting the hindered electrode process (Fig. 4c). The Tafel slope of CD/CNTs is 67.1 mV dec−1, which is smaller than that of CDs (113.8 mV dec−1), CNTs (85.8 mV dec−1) and 20 % Pt/C (72.5 mV dec−1), further confirming the excellent ORR performance of the CD/CNTs catalyst. Moreover, the Nyquist curve measured (Fig. 4d) shows that CD/CNTs (367 Ω) have less resistance than CNTs (10456 Ω), which indicates that CD/CNTs have faster charge transfer capability and indirectly implies that the synergistic effect between CDs and CNTs jointly promotes ORR activity [56–58].

To further comprehend the ORR electrocatalytic activity of CD/CNTs, the LSV of the rotating disk electrode (RDE) system was measured in a O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a scanning rate of 10 mV/s with various rotating speeds from 400 to 2500 rpm (Fig. 4e). Due to the increase in the electrolyte diffusion mass transfer rate, the limiting current density of the LSV curve increases with increasing rotation speed. The electron transfer number (n) is also one of the indicators used to evaluate the activity of ORR catalysts [59,60], and we can calculate n by the Kouteckye − Levich equation based on the data of Fig. 4e and Fig. S8. The catalyst has a good linear relationship in the potential range of 0.2–0.6 V, which conforms to the characteristics of first-order reaction kinetics, n = 3.9, which demonstrates that there is a 4 e- transfer process. The near-4 e- transfer process verifies the advantages of CD/CNTs carbon nanomaterials on ORR catalytic activity. Furthermore, the H2O2 yield and n of the CD/CNTs and 20 % Pt/C catalyst were measured by rotating ring-disk electrodes (RRDEs) (Fig. S9 and Fig. S10). The H2O2 yield of CDs/CNTs is less than 10 %, which is similar to that of 20 % Pt/C. Meanwhile, the calculated n of CD/CNTs is also close to 4, which further verifies that CD/CNTs exhibit the same transfer process as the 20 % Pt/C catalyst.

To evaluate the durability of the catalyst, the LSV curves were tested before and after 2000 cycles. As shown in Fig. S11, the shift of the E1/2 of the CD/CNTs (ΔE1/2 = -14.3 mV) is obviously smaller than that of 20 % Pt/C (ΔE1/2 = -21.4 mV), indicating the excellent stability of the CD/CNTs. The catalytic durability of CD/CNTs was further investigated by chronoamperometry in 0.1 M KOH solution. As shown in Fig. 4f, the CD/CNTs retained more than 90 % of the initial current (0.67 V vs RHE) after 30,000 s of continuous high-current operation, while the commercial 20 % Pt/C catalyst suffered from a huge loss and retained only 68 % at the same measurement condition. All of these results indicate that the formation of a nano heterojunction between CDs and CNTs endows the heterojunction with excellent stability. Its high electron cloud density improves the ORR limiting current density of the heterojunction [61–63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) test of the synthesized catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In general, ORR catalysts with excellent performance should have a good adsorption affinity for O2 [64–66]. The adsorbed O2 can strongly bind to the vacancy on the catalyst surface, which can trigger the elongation of the OO bond, thus reducing the activation energy and promoting the reduction of O2 [67–69]. To characterize the O2 adsorption capacity of the catalyst, O2-TPD tests were performed on CNTs and CD/CNTs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The CD/CNTs have a stronger O2 adsorption capacity/surface binding strength of O2 than CNTs, indicating that the introduction of CDs could significantly enhance the O2 adsorption capacity of CD/CNTs. Then, the increased O2 adsorption properties of CD/CNTs will promote the occurrence of ORR.

To further probe the acid-base sites of the catalyst, we programmed TPD measurements using CO2 and NH3 as probe molecules. In general, CO2 is often used to identify the Lewis basicity of the catalyst [70,71]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the CO2-TPD profiles of CNTs and CD/CNTs show a weak Lewis basicity to the CO2 adsorption site at 278 °C. Notably, the CD/CNTs exhibit a strong Lewis basicity to the CO2 adsorption site at 550 °C. It is generally believed that high-basicity sites are the key to promoting ORR activity [72]. We attributed the high Lewis basicity to the relatively high content of amino functional groups introduced by CDs on CNTs, which is consistent with the above-discussed results. Meanwhile, NH3-TPD was also used to test the Lewis acidity of the catalyst (Fig. S12). None of the CNTs and CD/CNTs had any response signal to NH3, indicating that the synthesized catalyst contained only a single Lewis base site.

In summary, surface state engineering was innovatively used to fabricate CDs anchored on spiral CNTs to form CD/CNT heterojunctions. While the monodispersed CDs anchored on CNTs, there was no obvious agglomeration on the surface CNTs and did not modify the original structure of the CNTs. The introduction of CDs with high amino-N content endowed CD/CNTs with a larger surface area and a large number of amino functional groups on their surface, which improved the hydrophilicity of their surface and led to the fracture of the CO group in the composite. The CD/CNTs with good hydrophilicity had excellent ORR activity (E1/2 = 0.81 V vs RHE, limiting current density = 4.74 mA cm−2 and Tafel slope = 67.1 mV dec−1). More importantly, the excellent long-term cycling performance of CD/CNTs was illustrated, whose current density can be maintained at 90 % in the I-T test as long as 30000 s. Combined with the analysis of the previous results, the excellent ORR activity of CD/CNTs was attributed to the dominance of amino nitrogen introduced by CDs on CNTs in the catalytic process, which was further illustrated by the CO2/O2-TPD test. Through surface state engineering, the composite was endowed with excellent hydrophilicity, a high content of amino functional groups, rich active sites and superior ORR activity. Our developed CD-regulated strategy will guide the rational design of advanced carbon-based reliable cathode electrodes for the next generation of energy storage and conversion equipment. In addition, the possible synergy between functional groups and architecture warrants further study.
Data will be made available on request.

Baohua Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Guangbin An: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jia Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Liang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21901154), Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.01.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen reduction electrocatalysts toward practical fuel cells: Progress and Perspectives
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zaman, L. Huang, A.I. Douka, H. Yang, B. You, B.Y. Xia, Oxygen reduction electrocatalysts toward practical fuel cells: Progress and Perspectives, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (33) (2021) 17832-17852.
